envs.label: Cursor
envs.hint: Cursor IDE + CLI in its terminal

categories.title: Getting started
categories.description: Installing Claude Code, signing in, starting your first session, and keeping the install healthy.

items.title: Install Claude Code
items.summary: Install the CLI and/or the IDE/desktop app.
items.details: Claude Code ships as a CLI (npm), a Mac/Windows desktop app, and IDE extensions for VS Code and JetBrains. The same account works across all of them.

cli.note: macOS / Linux / WSL one-liner — no Node prerequisite.

vscode.code: Extensions panel → search “Claude Code” → Install → sign in.

cursor.code:
# Use the CLI in Cursor's terminal
npm install -g @anthropic-ai/claude-code
claude
cursor.note: Cursor's own AI is a separate product.

desktop.code: Download from claude.com/claude-code → install → sign in.
desktop.note: Pairs with your local file system on first launch.

items.title: Sign in: Pro vs API
items.summary: Two billing paths — Claude Pro/Max subscription, or Anthropic API credits.
items.details: claude.ai login routes through Pro/Max. API key login (from console.anthropic.com) bills per-token. They're separate — Pro elsewhere doesn't auto-route Claude Code through Pro. Use /status to verify.
items.pitfalls: Most common surprise: being charged API credits when you have a Pro/Max subscription. Run /status the first time you set up — Pro on claude.ai doesn't automatically apply to Claude Code unless you signed in with that account.

examples.note: Pick a Pro/Max account or paste an API key. Switches at any time.
Confirms which account/auth path is active.

items.title: Start your first session
items.summary: Always launch from the project root — that becomes Claude's context.
items.details: The current working directory becomes the project context: file reads, edits, and shell commands are scoped to that tree. Run /init first to scaffold a CLAUDE.md.

cli.note: Default: launches an interactive session in this folder.
With an initial prompt — Claude starts with that instead of an empty REPL.
Pick a model at launch (haiku / sonnet / opus). Switch later with /model.

vscode.code: Open folder → click Claude icon → start chatting.

items.title: Update Claude Code
items.summary: Auto-updates by default; force a check with the npm package.
items.details: The CLI auto-updates in the background. Disable with the env var DISABLE_AUTOUPDATER=1 if you want to pin a version. IDE extensions update through the marketplace.
items.pitfalls: Auto-updates can change behavior mid-session. If you're shipping something time-sensitive or recording a demo, pin with DISABLE_AUTOUPDATER=1 in your env first.

examples.note: Check the installed version.
Force a fresh install.
Pin: skip the background auto-update for this session.

items.title: CLAUDE_CODE_NO_FLICKER
items.summary: Smoother terminal rendering — output streams line-by-line without screen redraws.
items.details: Set this env var (export it in your shell rc) to disable Claude Code's full-screen redraws in the terminal. Output flows linearly instead of repainting; easier on eyes, easier to scrollback through, easier to record.

examples.note: One session — set and re-run claude.
Persist for every shell. Swap ~/.zshrc for ~/.bashrc on bash.

items.title: Diagnose problems: /doctor
items.summary: One command to surface install, network, and permission issues.
items.details: Run when something feels off — wrong account, missing tools, network errors. /doctor reports Node version, install path, auth state, and config file locations.

example.note: Always paste this output when filing a bug.

categories.title: Sessions & modes
categories.description: How sessions work and the modes you can run them in — interactive REPL, plan-only, auto-accept, and headless one-shot.
categories.longDescription:
A "mode" is the policy that governs how Claude acts — whether it can edit files, ask before each step, or run unattended.

Reach for this section when a task feels mismatched to the default: use Plan mode before a risky refactor so Claude maps it out without touching code, Accept Edits mode when you trust the direction and want to stop confirming each save, or Print mode (-p) when you want a one-shot answer from the shell with no interactive loop.

Switch by typing /plan or /accept-edits, pressing Shift+Tab to cycle, or launching the CLI with the relevant flag. The right mode for the job is usually the most restrictive one that still gets the work done.

items.title: Interactive mode
items.summary: The default — back-and-forth REPL with tools and file edits.
items.details: Type messages, Claude can read files, run commands, edit code, search the web. Multi-line input via Shift+Enter (or paste). Esc to interrupt.

example.code:
$ claude
> add a /healthz endpoint and a test for it
example.note: Esc interrupts. Ctrl+C quits.

items.title: Plan mode
items.summary: Read-only — Claude proposes a plan but won't edit until you approve.
items.details: Use for non-trivial features or refactors. Plan mode disables Edit/Write/Bash. Cycle modes with Shift+Tab: Default → Accept Edits → Plan.

example.code:
Shift+Tab → Plan mode
> design how to add OAuth — don't implement
→ approve the plan to leave Plan mode
example.note: Or type /plan to enter directly.

items.title: Accept Edits mode
items.summary: Auto-approve file edits for the rest of the session.
items.details: Skips the per-edit confirmation. Shell commands and risky tools still ask. Useful when you trust the trajectory and want to stop clicking yes.

example.code:
Shift+Tab once → Accept Edits
(Edit/Write tools auto-approve; Bash still asks)
example.note: Cycle off with another Shift+Tab.

items.title: Print / headless mode (-p)
items.summary: One-shot, non-interactive — perfect for scripting and CI.
items.details: claude -p "prompt" runs the prompt, prints to stdout, exits. Inherits CLAUDE.md and settings. Combine with --output-format json for parseable results.
items.pitfalls: Inherits CLAUDE.md and the full settings stack — so the same prompt can behave differently in CI than on your laptop. Always specify --permission-mode and --max-turns explicitly in CI; otherwise a hidden interactive prompt can hang the run.

examples.note: One-shot. Stdout is the answer.

examples.code: claude -p "is this diff safe?" --output-format json | jq .result
examples.note: Pipe-friendly: parseable JSON output.

items.title: Resume a session
items.summary: Pick up where you left off — full context restored.
items.details: claude --continue (or -c) reopens the last session in the cwd. claude --resume opens an interactive picker. You can resume in headless mode too.

examples.note: Continue the last session in this folder. Sessions are scoped per-folder.
Open the interactive picker to choose a past session.
Resume + headless: continue silently and run a one-shot prompt.

items.title: --max-turns / --max-budget-usd
items.summary: Bound how many turns — or how many dollars — a headless run may use.
items.details: Two complementary caps for headless mode. `--max-turns N` limits the number of agentic turns; `--max-budget-usd X` limits cumulative cost in USD. Either condition triggers a clean exit. Use both in CI to prevent runaway loops.
items.pitfalls: Both exit the moment the cap hits — even mid-tool-call. Design prompts so the work-in-progress at any cutoff is still useful (e.g. produce a partial diff and a TODO list, not just the final answer).

examples.note: Bound the number of agentic turns.
Bound cumulative cost (USD). Either cap triggers exit.

items.title: Custom system prompt
items.summary: Override or append to the default Claude Code system prompt.
items.details: --system-prompt replaces the default. --append-system-prompt adds to it. Use sparingly — Claude Code's default prompt encodes a lot of safety and tool-use behavior.

example.note: Often paired with stream-json output for orchestrators.

categories.title: Slash commands
categories.description: Built-in commands typed with a leading / inside a session — control the session, inspect state, manage memory, run workflows.
categories.longDescription:
Slash commands are the control panel for an active session — anything the harness can do that you'd otherwise have to remember is exposed as /something.

Open this section when you forget how to clear context, switch model, see costs, review a diff, or open settings without leaving the prompt. They also cover the small but high-leverage ones: /btw for a side question that doesn't pollute context, /rewind to undo a turn, /add-dir to grant access outside the cwd.

Type the command with / at the start of any message; /help lists everything available right now (including custom commands you've added under .claude/commands/). Many take arguments — e.g. /compact <topic>, /review <PR#>, /model opus.

items.title: /help
items.summary: List commands, shortcuts, and active skills.
items.details: Your first stop when you forget something.

items.title: /clear
items.summary: Wipe the current conversation; memory and CLAUDE.md remain.
items.details: Resets context only — settings, memory, and connections survive.
items.pitfalls: Doesn't clear CLAUDE.md or settings. If Claude is still doing something you didn't ask for after /clear, the rule lives in CLAUDE.md (or a hook), not the conversation.

example.note: Aliases: /reset, /new

items.title: /exit
items.summary: Quit the session.
items.details: Same as Ctrl+C on an empty prompt.

example.note: Alias: /quit

items.title: /btw
items.summary: Ask a quick side question without consuming main context.
items.details: Spawns an isolated mini-conversation: ask, get an answer, return to your main session unchanged. Great for a one-off lookup mid-task without blowing your context budget.

example.note: The reply doesn't enter your main conversation history.

items.title: /simplify
items.summary: Built-in skill: review recently changed files for quality and efficiency.
items.details: Bundled skill that scans your recent edits and proposes simpler, cleaner alternatives — dead code, redundant abstractions, naming that drifted. Pairs nicely with /review.

example.note: Lists items with file:line and a one-sentence rationale per suggestion.

items.title: /init
items.summary: Generate a CLAUDE.md by analyzing the current repo.
items.details: Writes a project-level CLAUDE.md with build/test/lint commands and architectural notes.
items.pitfalls: The generated CLAUDE.md is a first draft, not the finished article. Edit before relying on it — early passes often miss your real conventions and include generic advice you don't actually want enforced.

example.note: Edit the result — first pass is a starting point.

items.title: /status
items.summary: Show account, model, working dir, and session info.
items.details: Quickest way to confirm Pro vs API and which model is active.

items.title: /compact
items.summary: Summarize old turns to free up context.
items.details: Replaces older turns with a condensed summary. Optional argument biases the summary toward a topic.
items.pitfalls: Auto-compaction near the context limit can drop details Claude needed mid-task. If a session feels suddenly amnesiac, /context shows what survived; rebuild critical info with #-memory or @-references rather than re-explaining.

examples.note: Default: summarize older turns. Auto-runs near the context limit.
Bias the summary toward a topic.

items.title: /model
items.summary: Switch between Opus, Sonnet, and Haiku mid-session.
items.details: Opus = strongest, Sonnet = great default, Haiku = fastest/cheapest. Switching keeps context.

examples.note: Opens the picker.
Set the model directly without the picker.

items.title: /memory
items.summary: Open or edit the CLAUDE.md files in scope.
items.details: Quick access to project + user CLAUDE.md without leaving the session.

example.note: Use # at the start of a message for a one-off save.

items.title: /review
items.summary: Review the current diff or a GitHub PR.
items.details: Without args, reviews uncommitted changes. With a PR number, fetches and reviews that PR.

examples.note: Review the local diff (uncommitted changes).
Fetch GitHub PR #1234 and review it. Pairs well with /security-review.

items.title: /agents
items.summary: List, create, and edit sub-agents.
items.details: Browse built-in agents (Explore, Plan, general-purpose) and your own from .claude/agents/.

items.title: /mcp
items.summary: Manage MCP server connections.
items.details: Show connected servers, their tools, and re-authenticate or restart them.

items.title: /skills
items.summary: List skills available in this session.
items.details: Includes built-in skills and your own from .claude/skills/. Skills can also auto-activate.

items.title: /config
items.summary: Open settings.json in your editor.
items.details: Picks the right scope (user vs project) for what you're editing.

example.note: Alias: /settings

items.title: /cost
items.summary: Show token usage and spend for this session.
items.details: Per-model breakdown of input/output tokens and cache stats.

example.note: Pro/Max sessions also show plan-relative usage.

items.title: /add-dir
items.summary: Grant Claude file access to an additional directory.
items.details: By default Claude can only see the cwd. Use /add-dir to extend scope (e.g., a sibling repo).

items.title: /rewind
items.summary: Roll the conversation (and optionally code) back to a checkpoint.
items.details: Pick a previous user turn; the session reverts. Optionally also reverts file edits made since.

example.note: Esc Esc is a shortcut to edit the last prompt.

items.title: /diff
items.summary: Interactive viewer for uncommitted changes and per-turn diffs.
items.details: Side-by-side or unified view of edits Claude has made.

items.title: /copy
items.summary: Copy the last response (or a code block) to clipboard.
items.details: Optionally pick a numbered code block from the response.

items.title: /export
items.summary: Save the conversation to a file or clipboard.
items.details: Markdown by default. Useful for sharing repros or PR descriptions.

items.title: /context
items.summary: Visualize what's loaded in the context window.
items.details: Breakdown of system prompt, CLAUDE.md, tool defs, conversation, and free space.

example.note: Use before /compact to see what to drop.

items.title: /usage
items.summary: Show plan limits and current rate-limit status.
items.details: On Pro/Max plans, shows window utilization and reset time.

items.title: /hooks
items.summary: View hook configuration for tool events.
items.details: Shows which hooks fire on which events, with their commands and matchers.

items.title: /permissions
items.summary: View or change which tools require approval.
items.details: Inspect allow/deny rules and the matchers that trigger them.

items.title: /logout
items.summary: Sign out of the current account.
items.details: Useful to switch between Pro and API credentials.

items.title: /bug
items.summary: Report an issue to Anthropic with session context.
items.details: Bundles redacted state — model, OS, last turns — for a clean repro.

example.note: Alias: /feedback

items.title: /loop
items.summary: Run a prompt or slash command on an interval.
items.details: /loop 5m /babysit-prs runs every 5 minutes. Drop the interval to let the model self-pace. Stop with /loop-stop.

examples.code: /loop 5m check the deploy and ping me on failure
examples.note: With an interval (5m): re-runs the prompt every 5 minutes.
No interval: model self-paces. Stop with /loop-stop.

items.title: /schedule
items.summary: Cron-scheduled remote agents that run while you sleep.
items.details: Cloud-side routines on a cron — one-time follow-ups or recurring work.

examples.code: /schedule weekly Mondays 9am — open a PR removing closed flags
examples.note: Recurring routine.

examples.code: /schedule once in 14 days — clean up the BETA_ROLLOUT gate
examples.note: One-time follow-up. Manage all routines with `/schedule list`.

categories.title: Memory & CLAUDE.md
categories.description: How Claude carries instructions across sessions. CLAUDE.md files at the user, project, and sub-folder level compose into a single set of standing rules.
categories.longDescription:
CLAUDE.md is how you make rules stick across sessions — anything you'd otherwise have to repeat in every prompt belongs here.

Use this section when Claude keeps making the same mistake, when onboarding a new repo, or when you want the same standards to apply to everyone on the team. Typical contents: build/test commands, naming conventions, files to ignore, security rules, links to architecture docs.

Files are layered and merge automatically: ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md for personal preferences, <repo>/CLAUDE.md (committed) for project conventions, and per-folder CLAUDE.md for narrow scope (e.g. only when editing inside /api). Open them with /memory, or prefix a single message with # to file a one-off rule into the right scope. Keep them short — every line costs context tokens on every turn.

items.title: Project CLAUDE.md
items.summary: Per-repo instructions — commands, conventions, architecture.
items.details: Lives at the repo root. Loaded into every session in that folder. Keep it tight: build/test/lint commands, big-picture architecture, non-obvious conventions.

example.code:
# CLAUDE.md

## Commands
- Build: `pnpm build`
- Test: `pnpm test --run`

## Architecture
Next.js front end + Go gRPC backend.
example.note: Run /init to scaffold.

items.title: User CLAUDE.md (global)
items.summary: Personal instructions that apply to every project.
items.details: Lives at ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md. Use for personal preferences, identity, recurring instructions you don't want to repeat per repo.

example.code:
# Global
- Always mark generated files with: © Your Name
- Prefer explicit TS types over inference
- Don't add backwards-compat shims unless I ask
example.note: Loaded in addition to project CLAUDE.md.

items.title: Save instruction with #
items.summary: Start a message with # to save it to CLAUDE.md.
items.details: Quick way to teach Claude a convention permanently. Claude asks which CLAUDE.md (project vs user) and appends.

example.code: # always run pnpm test --run, never watch mode

items.title: Imports inside CLAUDE.md
items.summary: Compose CLAUDE.md from smaller files with @path imports.
items.details: @path/to/file.md inside a CLAUDE.md inlines that file. Useful for shared standards across repos or sub-package overrides.

example.note: Imports are resolved at session start.

items.title: Memory hierarchy
items.summary: How user, project, and sub-folder CLAUDE.md files compose.
items.details: Claude reads user (~/.claude), the repo root, and any ancestor folder above the cwd. Closer = higher precedence on conflicts.

example.code:
~/.claude/CLAUDE.md          ← global
repo/CLAUDE.md               ← project
repo/services/api/CLAUDE.md  ← only when cwd is inside services/api
example.note: Most-specific wins.

categories.title: Quick references & shortcuts
categories.description: Inline syntax (@, #, !) and keybindings that make daily use fast — file references, memory shortcuts, shell escapes, and mode toggles.
categories.longDescription:
These are the inline characters and keystrokes that turn a slow conversation into a fast one — @ to attach a file, # to file a memory, ! to escape to the shell, plus the keybindings for plan/accept/cancel and the search/help overlays.

Use this section when you find yourself typing long file paths into prompts, repeating the same instruction across sessions, double-pressing Esc to undo, or reaching for the mouse to do something the keyboard already supports.

There's nothing to install — these all work the moment you start typing. Worth memorizing the top five (@, #, !, Shift+Tab, Esc-Esc) before going deeper; they pay back the time within a session or two.

items.title: @ file reference
items.summary: @path/to/file includes the file's contents in your prompt.
items.details: Tab-completes paths. Tokenizes better than pasting and shows up in the audit log as a real reference.

items.title: @ directory reference
items.summary: @path/to/dir/ pulls in the whole directory.
items.details: Skips files in .gitignore. Use sparingly — directories can blow your context.

items.title: @ URL reference
items.summary: @https://example.com fetches a URL (read-only).
items.details: Pulls the page once and includes it as context. Honors robots.txt.

items.title: # save to CLAUDE.md
items.summary: A leading # in your message saves it as a memory.
items.details: Claude asks which CLAUDE.md to save into. Faster than /memory for one-off rules.

items.title: ! shell escape
items.summary: A leading ! runs the rest as a shell command.
items.details: Output is shown to you and fed back into Claude's context. Faster than asking Claude to run something.

examples.note: Quick: show working tree.
Targeted: run one test file. Output flows into Claude's context.

items.title: Paste an image
items.summary: Cmd/Ctrl+V a screenshot directly into the prompt.
items.details: Vision-capable models (Sonnet, Opus) read screenshots, design mockups, error dialogs, etc.

example.code:
[Paste screenshot]
What's wrong with this layout?

items.title: Shift+Tab — cycle modes
items.summary: Toggle Default → Accept Edits → Plan.
items.details: Visible mode indicator updates in the prompt. Shift+Tab again to cycle.

items.title: Esc Esc — rewind & edit
items.summary: Double-Esc to jump back and edit your previous prompt.
items.details: Faster than /rewind for the common case of fixing a typo or refining intent.

example.note: Single Esc interrupts a running response.

items.title: Ctrl+R — verbose transcript
items.summary: Expand the full transcript including tool calls and outputs.
items.details: Helpful for debugging what Claude actually did vs. what it summarized.

items.title: ↑ / ↓ — prompt history
items.summary: Scroll through previous prompts; Tab completes paths.
items.details: History is per-folder.

categories.title: Hooks
categories.description: Shell commands the harness runs at lifecycle events. Use them for auto-formatting, logging, blocking unsafe calls, or notifications — anything you don't want to trust the model to remember.
categories.longDescription:
Hooks are shell commands the harness runs automatically at lifecycle events — before a tool call, after a file edit, when the session starts or ends, when Claude is waiting on you.

Use them when you don't want to trust the model to remember something: auto-format every file on save, log every Bash invocation to a file, block writes to .env or production paths, fire a desktop notification when Claude finishes a long run, or inject extra context at session start.

Configure under the hooks key in settings.json (project, user, or local scope). A hook that exits non-zero blocks the action and surfaces stderr to Claude — useful for safety gates. Because hooks run shell, they should be small, fast, and idempotent; reach for a sub-agent or skill instead when the logic is non-trivial.

items.title: Hook events — quick reference
items.summary: All hook events at a glance: when they fire, what's in the payload, how to override behavior.
items.details: Use this as a lookup when wiring a new hook and you've forgotten which event matches the trigger you want, what data your script will see, or how to influence the outcome. Drill into the individual event items below for full examples.

rows: Stdout JSON can rewrite the prompt
table.footnote: All hooks receive event JSON on STDIN — parse with jq or `read`. Configure under `hooks` in settings.json. The matcher (where supported) is a regex over the tool name, e.g. `"Bash|Write"`.

example.note: Drop into ~/.claude/settings.json (user) or .claude/settings.json (project).

items.title: What hooks are
items.summary: Shell commands the harness runs at lifecycle events, with event JSON on stdin.
items.details: Configured under hooks in settings.json. Each hook is a command. Non-zero exit on PreToolUse blocks the call. JSON written to stdout can override outcomes for advanced use.
items.pitfalls: Hooks receive event JSON on STDIN, not as command arguments — the #1 reason a hook 'silently does nothing' is reading $1 instead of stdin. Parse with jq or `read -r line` and operate on the parsed payload.

items.title: PreToolUse
items.summary: Inspect or block a tool call before it runs.
items.details: Exit non-zero to block. Match on tool name with regex (e.g., "Bash|Write").
items.pitfalls: Block via exit code OR JSON output (`{"decision":"block","reason":"…"}`) — pick one, mixing the two is undefined. Hooks fire on every matching tool call regardless of prior approvals; an over-eager matcher will pile up prompts.

example.note: stdin = JSON event with tool name + args.

items.title: PostToolUse
items.summary: React after a tool ran — log, format, notify.
items.details: Common pattern: auto-format edited files. Failures here don't roll back the tool, just log.

items.title: UserPromptSubmit
items.summary: Intercept the user's prompt before Claude sees it.
items.details: Inject context, redact secrets, or block on policy violations. JSON output can rewrite the prompt.

example.note: Hook stdout (JSON) can replace prompt text.

items.title: Stop / SubagentStop
items.summary: Fires when a turn finishes — desktop notifications, run tests, etc.
items.details: Stop is the main agent's turn-end. SubagentStop fires when a sub-agent completes.

items.title: SessionStart / SessionEnd
items.summary: Run setup/cleanup at the edges of a session.
items.details: Warm caches, pre-fetch tickets, log session boundaries, gather telemetry.

items.title: PreCompact
items.summary: Customize what gets summarized vs. dropped at /compact.
items.details: Hook receives the conversation; can return guidance for the summarizer.

items.title: Notification
items.summary: Fires when Claude is waiting on you (idle prompt).
items.details: Great for desktop pings when Claude needs input — no more checking the terminal.

items.title: Hook JSON output
items.summary: Write JSON to stdout to control hook outcomes.
items.details: Decision fields like { "decision": "block", "reason": "..." } let hooks return rich responses instead of just exit codes.

example.note: Documented in the hooks reference.

categories.title: MCP servers
categories.description: Model Context Protocol — an open standard for plugging external systems (GitHub, Linear, databases, your own APIs) into Claude as callable tools.
categories.longDescription:
MCP (Model Context Protocol) is the open standard for plugging external systems — GitHub, Linear, Slack, Postgres, your own internal APIs — into Claude as callable tools. Once a server is connected, its tools appear alongside built-ins and Claude can use them like any other.

Reach for this section when Claude needs to do work outside the local repo: read a Linear ticket, comment on a PR, query a database, post to a channel, or call a service you wrote. Anything that lives behind an API and you'd rather not paste manually is a candidate.

Install a server once with /mcp (or by editing mcpServers in settings.json), authenticate, and the tools show up in every future session. Project-scoped servers commit with the repo so the team gets them automatically; user-scoped servers stay personal. Vet what you connect — MCP tools can read and write on your behalf.

items.title: What is MCP?
items.summary: Open protocol for exposing tools/resources to LLMs.
items.details: Claude Code can connect to any MCP server — local or remote — and call its tools alongside built-ins.

example.note: Lists connected servers and their tools.

items.title: Add a local (stdio) MCP server
items.summary: Spawn a server as a subprocess and talk to it over stdio.
items.details: Works for any package that exposes an MCP CLI. Configure in settings.json under mcpServers.
items.pitfalls: env values aren't auto-inherited from your shell — `${VAR}` is interpolated but bare names pass through literally. Restart Claude Code after editing settings.json; running servers don't pick up changes.

items.title: Add a remote (HTTP/SSE) MCP server
items.summary: Connect to a hosted MCP server over HTTP.
items.details: Streamable HTTP / SSE transport. OAuth flows handled by the harness.

example.note: /mcp triggers the auth dance.

items.title: MCP scopes
items.summary: User vs project vs local — pick where the server lives.
items.details: User: everywhere. Project: shared with team (committed). Local: machine-only (gitignored). Local for secrets.

example.note: Settings deep-merge; local wins.

items.title: Per-tool MCP permissions
items.summary: Allow/deny rules with mcp__ prefix.
items.details: Match an MCP tool by mcp__<server>__<tool> in permission rules.
items.pitfalls: MCP tool names use double underscores: `mcp__github__create_issue`. Single-underscore matchers fail silently and never match. Verify by running /mcp — the listed tool names are exactly what permission rules need.

items.title: Debug an MCP server
items.summary: /mcp shows logs and connection state.
items.details: Restart, re-auth, view stderr from stdio servers, inspect available tools.

example.note: Pick a server to drill in.

categories.title: Sub-agents & skills
categories.description: Task-specific helpers Claude can spawn. Sub-agents run in their own context and report back; skills are reusable invocable workflows.
categories.longDescription:
Sub-agents and skills are how you teach Claude *playbooks*. A sub-agent is a scoped helper that runs in a fresh context (so its searches and reads don't pollute your main conversation) and returns a single summary; a skill is a reusable workflow that auto-activates when its description matches what the user is asking for.

Use this section when a task is large or repetitive enough to deserve its own definition: a code-reviewer that always checks the same things, a migration-checker for SQL safety, a release-notes generator, a debugger that follows a fixed reproduce/isolate/diagnose loop. Sub-agents shine on big read-heavy investigations; skills shine on repeatable procedures triggered by intent.

Define them as Markdown files under .claude/agents/ or .claude/skills/ with frontmatter (name, description, tools, model). Routing is matched on the description, so write that like a sentence the user would actually say ("audits SQL migrations for safety on large tables") rather than a feature list.

items.title: Explore agent
items.summary: Fast, read-only search across the repo.
items.details: Spawns a sub-agent for keyword/file lookups. Returns a tight summary without polluting your main context.

example.code: Use Explore to find where the rate-limiter lives.

items.title: Plan agent
items.summary: Design an implementation plan without writing code.
items.details: Ideal for non-trivial features — produces a step-by-step plan and identifies critical files.

example.code: Have the Plan agent design how to add OAuth — don't implement.

items.title: general-purpose agent
items.summary: Multi-step research and execution — when one focused agent isn't enough.
items.details: Has access to all tools. Use for open-ended investigations.

example.code: Use a general-purpose agent to research and migrate our test runner.

items.title: Custom sub-agents
items.summary: Define your own agents in .claude/agents/<name>.md.
items.details: Markdown file with frontmatter (name, description, tools, model). Claude routes to it when the description matches.
items.pitfalls: Routing keys off the `description` field, not the file name. Vague descriptions mean Claude never spawns the agent. Phrase the description like a sentence the user might say ("audits SQL migrations for safety on large tables") so the model can match intent.

example.code:
---
name: migration-checker
description: Audits a SQL migration for safety on large tables.
tools: Read, Bash, Grep
model: opus
---

You are a database safety reviewer...
example.note: .claude/agents/migration-checker.md

items.title: Worktree-isolated agents
items.summary: Run a sub-agent on a temporary git worktree to keep changes isolated.
items.details: Spawn the agent with isolation: "worktree" — it works in its own branch and merges or returns its diff cleanly.
items.pitfalls: Failed runs leave their worktree behind. `git worktree list` to see them; `git worktree remove <path>` to clean up. Branches accumulate too — prune periodically or your repo's branch list gets messy.

example.code: Spawn a sub-agent (worktree-isolated) to refactor the logger and report back.
example.note: Agent tool's isolation flag.

items.title: Built-in skills
items.summary: Invocable workflows like /init, /review, /security-review, /simplify.
items.details: Skills are bundled instructions + helpers. Some auto-activate when relevant; all are listed in /skills.

items.title: Custom skills
items.summary: Drop a SKILL.md in .claude/skills/<name>/ to add your own.
items.details: Skills can include helper scripts/files. They appear in /skills and can auto-activate when their description matches the user's intent.

example.code:
# .claude/skills/release-notes/SKILL.md
---
name: release-notes
description: Generate release notes from the merged PRs since the last tag.
---

When invoked, gather merged PRs since the latest tag and...

items.title: Custom slash commands
items.summary: Markdown files in .claude/commands/ become /-commands.
items.details: Frontmatter sets allowed-tools, model, args. The body is the prompt template. User scope (~/.claude/commands/) is shared across projects.
items.pitfalls: Frontmatter typos (`allowed_tools` vs `allowed-tools`, `Model` vs `model`) are ignored silently — the command runs without those tools and the model can't actually do anything. Validate with /skills or by running the command and watching what tools it tries to use.

example.code:
# .claude/commands/deploy.md
---
allowed-tools: Bash(git:*), Bash(./deploy.sh:*)
model: sonnet
---
Deploy {{1}} to staging and report the URL.
example.note: Then: /deploy v1.4

categories.title: Permissions & settings
categories.description: settings.json controls hooks, permissions, env vars, and MCP servers. Three scopes — user, project (committed), local (gitignored) — deep-merge in that order.
categories.longDescription:
settings.json is the single file that controls everything outside the conversation: which Bash commands are allowed without prompting, which hooks fire, which MCP servers are connected, which env vars Claude sees.

Use this section to stop the permission prompts you keep saying "yes" to (allow), lock down a destructive command for safety (deny), share a project's setup with the team (commit .claude/settings.json), or keep secrets and machine-specific paths off the repo (.claude/settings.local.json, gitignored).

The three scopes — user (~/.claude/settings.json), project (.claude/settings.json), and local (.claude/settings.local.json) — deep-merge in that order, so per-project rules override personal ones, and local overrides both. Edit with /config, or hand-edit the file. Permission patterns use globs (Bash(npm *), Read(./src/**)) — start narrow, widen as you trust a command.

items.title: settings.json basics
items.summary: One JSON file controls hooks, permissions, env, and MCP.
items.details: Three scopes: user (~/.claude/settings.json), project (<repo>/.claude/settings.json — committed), local (<repo>/.claude/settings.local.json — gitignored). Deep-merge: local > project > user.

example.note: /config opens the right scope for you.

items.title: Permission allow rules
items.summary: Auto-approve safe operations so you stop clicking yes.
items.details: Use prefix patterns. Bash matchers can use globs and parentheses to scope to argument prefixes.
items.pitfalls: Broad globs like Bash(git:*) silently include destructive variants — git reset --hard, git push --force. Whitelist specific sub-commands (Bash(git status), Bash(git diff:*)) instead, and pair with deny/ask rules for the dangerous variants.

example.note: Keep destructive commands out of allow.

items.title: Permission deny rules
items.summary: Hard-block specific operations regardless of allow rules.
items.details: Deny wins over allow. Use for rm -rf, force pushes, secret reads, prod deploys.
items.pitfalls: Deny rules match how the model invokes the command, not how a human would. Bash(rm:*) blocks `rm` but not `sudo rm`, not `find … -delete`, not `bash -c 'rm …'`. Layer matchers and pair with a PreToolUse hook for context-aware checks.

items.title: ask vs allow
items.summary: ask forces a prompt even when a parent rule would allow.
items.details: Use to gate sensitive subsets of an otherwise-trusted family of commands.

items.title: env: inject environment variables
items.summary: Pass env into every Claude session in scope.
items.details: Useful for ANTHROPIC_API_KEY, NODE_OPTIONS, proxies, debug flags. Keep secrets in settings.local.json, not the committed project file.

items.title: settings.local.json
items.summary: Per-machine overrides — gitignored, safe for secrets.
items.details: Same shape as settings.json. Merges last so it wins ties. Add to .gitignore (Claude Code does this automatically).

items.title: Custom status line
items.summary: Replace the default prompt status line with your own.
items.details: statusLine.command runs each turn; its stdout becomes the prompt's status line. Show git branch, model, cost, anything.

items.title: Output styles
items.summary: Bias responses toward verbose, concise, or custom voices.
items.details: outputStyles in settings selects from built-in styles or your own .claude/output-styles/<name>.md.

items.title: Telemetry & data location
items.summary: Where transcripts, sessions, and logs live locally.
items.details: Sessions stored under ~/.claude/projects/<project>/. Set DISABLE_TELEMETRY=1 to opt out of optional analytics.

categories.title: IDE integration
categories.description: Native experiences inside VS Code and JetBrains, plus how to use the CLI from Cursor's terminal.
categories.longDescription:
The IDE integration gives Claude access to your editor's selection, open tabs, diagnostics, and side-panel UI — diffs render inline, file references resolve to the right tab, and you can drive a session without leaving the keyboard.

Use this section when you want diff views in your editor instead of a terminal, want to highlight a region and ask about it, want JetBrains/VS Code shortcuts to trigger Claude, or want diagnostics (TS errors, lint warnings) to flow into the conversation automatically.

Install the VS Code or JetBrains extension once from the marketplace; the CLI auto-detects when it's launched from inside an IDE terminal. In Cursor, there's no separate extension — open Cursor's integrated terminal and run claude as you would anywhere else; the editor selection is still picked up via @-references.

items.title: Features × environments — what works where
items.summary: At-a-glance view of which Claude Code features run in which environment.
items.details: If you're picking an environment to commit to (or considering switching), this matrix shows where each feature is available natively, where it's available through a workaround, and where it's not.

table.footnote: Cursor doesn't ship Claude Code; the column tracks features available when you run the `claude` CLI inside Cursor's terminal. Cursor's own AI (Composer / Cmd-K) is a separate product.

example.code:
Rule of thumb:
  CLI         — most powerful (every feature, scriptable)
  Desktop     — best general-purpose UI
  VS Code     — best in-editor experience (inline diffs)
  Cursor      — Claude Code via terminal + Cursor's own AI in editor
example.note: Pick by where you spend your day — features other than inline-diff are essentially identical across environments.

items.title: VS Code extension
items.summary: Native chat panel + inline diffs.
items.details: Cmd/Ctrl+Esc opens the panel. Inline diffs apply directly to the editor; accept/reject hunk-by-hunk.

example.code:
Cmd/Ctrl+Esc       — toggle panel
Cmd/Ctrl+L         — send selection as context

items.title: VS Code shortcuts & gotchas
items.summary: Selections auto-share; settings live in the same .claude folder.
items.details: Selection in the editor is included on send. The extension reads the same settings.json as the CLI — no separate config.

example.code: Highlight code → Cmd+Esc → ask. Selection is the context.

items.title: JetBrains plugin
items.summary: IntelliJ / PyCharm / WebStorm / GoLand support.
items.details: Same model + account as the CLI. Plugin docks into the IDE.

example.code: Settings → Plugins → search “Claude Code” → Install → Restart

items.title: Claude Code in Cursor
items.summary: Two paths: run the CLI in Cursor's terminal, or use Cursor's own AI.
items.details: Cursor doesn't ship Claude Code. The CLI works in Cursor's terminal. Cursor's Composer/Cmd-K is a separate AI integration.

cursor.note: Editor selections aren't auto-shared with the CLI.

items.title: MCP & hooks work everywhere
items.summary: MCP servers, hooks, and skills are environment-agnostic.
items.details: The CLI and the IDE extensions read the same settings.json. Configure once, use anywhere.

example.code: Edit ~/.claude/settings.json → effective in CLI, VS Code, JetBrains.

categories.title: Headless & scripting
categories.description: Running Claude one-shot from the shell — git hooks, CI gates, pipelines. Use -p, --output-format, --max-turns.
categories.longDescription:
"Headless" means running Claude one-shot from the shell — no interactive loop, no manual approvals — so it can be wired into git hooks, CI gates, cron jobs, and any pipeline that runs unattended.

Use this section when you want Claude in something automated: a pre-commit lint pass, a PR comment that flags risky migrations, a nightly summary of failing tests, a script that reads stdin and writes a structured response. The mental model: same prompts and skills as interactive mode, just one turn that returns and exits.

The core flag is -p "your prompt" (alias for --print). Pair it with --output-format json for parseable output, --max-turns and --max-budget-usd to bound the run, and --dangerously-skip-permissions only inside an isolated sandbox you trust. Pipe stdin in for input, parse stdout/JSON downstream. CI tip: run the same prompt in plan mode first to inspect what it would do before letting it write.

items.title: claude -p basics
items.summary: One-shot prompts from the shell.
items.details: Inherits CLAUDE.md and settings. Stdout is the answer; no interactive prompts.

items.title: Pipe stdin into claude -p
items.summary: Feed any command's output into Claude as the prompt's data.
items.details: Anything on stdin gets prepended to the prompt. Combine with -p for one-line analysis: logs, diffs, jq output, ps listings, anything text-shaped.

examples.code: git log --oneline | claude -p "summarize what changed this week"
examples.note: Summarize recent commits.

examples.code: cat error.log | claude -p "find the root cause"
examples.note: Triage logs without copy-paste.

examples.code: kubectl get pods | claude -p "any pod looks unhealthy?"
examples.note: Anything text-shaped works.

items.title: --output-format json
items.summary: Parseable structured output for pipelines.
items.details: Returns turns, tool calls, and metadata as JSON. Pipe through jq.

example.code:
claude -p "are there secrets in this diff?" \
  --output-format json | jq -r '.result'

items.title: --output-format stream-json
items.summary: Newline-delimited JSON events as Claude works.
items.details: Stream tool calls and tokens to a watcher. Ideal for orchestrators and dashboards.

example.note: Each line is a discrete event.

items.title: Resume in headless mode
items.summary: Continue a previous conversation non-interactively.
items.details: Use --resume <session-id> for a specific session, or -c to continue the latest in cwd.

examples.note: Resume a specific session by id.
Continue the latest session in this folder.

items.title: Headless permissions
items.summary: --permission-mode controls how strict prompts are in -p.
items.details: auto-accepts edits or runs in plan mode without UI. For CI, pair with explicit allow/deny rules.
items.pitfalls: Without explicit allow rules, headless runs hang on a prompt that no human will ever answer. Always set `--permission-mode acceptEdits` (or `plan`) AND pre-allow the tools you need in settings.json. Also set --max-turns so a stuck loop doesn't bill forever.

items.title: Pattern: prepare-commit-msg hook
items.summary: Auto-generate commit messages from staged diffs.
items.details: Drop a script in .git/hooks/prepare-commit-msg that calls claude -p.

example.note: Make executable.

categories.title: Integrations & deployments
categories.description: Running Claude Code beyond your laptop — GitHub Actions, the Anthropic and Agent SDKs, and Bedrock/Vertex backends.
categories.longDescription:
These are the ways to run Claude Code *beyond your laptop* — GitHub Actions for in-PR automation, the Anthropic and Agent SDKs for embedding Claude into your own apps, and AWS Bedrock or Google Vertex when keys must stay in your cloud account.

Use this section when you're going from a local tool to a deployed one: a bot that comments on every PR, a custom agent inside an internal product, a scheduled action that opens follow-up issues, or an enterprise rollout where data and credentials must stay inside your VPC.

Each option uses the same prompts, hooks, skills, and CLAUDE.md you wrote locally — only the runtime changes. GitHub Actions: drop in the official action and it runs headless on each event. SDKs: programmatic access to the same model and tools. Bedrock/Vertex: set the relevant env var (CLAUDE_CODE_USE_BEDROCK, CLAUDE_CODE_USE_VERTEX) and Claude routes inference through your account.

items.title: GitHub Actions: claude-code-action
items.summary: Run Claude Code from CI on PRs, issues, or schedules.
items.details: anthropics/claude-code-action handles auth, lets you wire reviews, autofixes, or scheduled tasks.

example.code:
- uses: anthropics/claude-code-action@v1
  with:
    anthropic_api_key: ${{ secrets.ANTHROPIC_API_KEY }}
    prompt: |
      Review the diff in this PR and post comments inline.

items.title: Claude Agent SDK
items.summary: Programmatically spawn Claude Code-style agents in your own apps.
items.details: TypeScript and Python SDKs. Same tools, hooks, and MCP support — wired into your code.

items.title: Anthropic SDK
items.summary: Direct API access — for fully custom agents and integrations.
items.details: @anthropic-ai/sdk (TS) and anthropic (Python). Lower-level than the Agent SDK; you build the loop yourself.

items.title: AWS Bedrock backend
items.summary: Route Claude Code calls through Bedrock for enterprise compliance.
items.details: Set CLAUDE_CODE_USE_BEDROCK=1 plus AWS creds. Model IDs use Bedrock's namespacing.
items.pitfalls: Model IDs don't match the Anthropic API namespace, and not every Claude version is available in every region. Look up the exact ID in the Bedrock console for your AWS_REGION before pinning a model in settings.

items.title: Google Vertex backend
items.summary: Route through Vertex AI for GCP-native deployments.
items.details: Set CLAUDE_CODE_USE_VERTEX=1 plus GCP project/region. Uses Vertex's model naming.

example.code:
export CLAUDE_CODE_USE_VERTEX=1
export CLOUD_ML_REGION=us-east5
export ANTHROPIC_VERTEX_PROJECT_ID=my-gcp-project
claude

items.title: Self-hosted proxy / BYO endpoint
items.summary: Point Claude Code at a custom Anthropic-compatible endpoint.
items.details: ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL lets you front the API with a proxy for logging, redaction, or routing.

categories.title: Workflow recipes
categories.description: End-to-end patterns that combine multiple features — hooks, headless mode, MCP, scheduled agents — into something useful in one go.
categories.longDescription:
Recipes are end-to-end patterns that wire several features together into something useful — a hook that runs eslint on every save and feeds errors back to Claude, a headless agent triggered nightly by cron, an MCP server consumed by a sub-agent, a /loop watcher that polls a build until it's green.

Use this section when you've got a problem in mind and want a working starting point rather than a primitive. Each recipe shows the moving parts (which hook, which command, which settings.json keys) so you can copy, swap the specifics for yours, and iterate.

Recipes are deliberately a notch above "single feature" tutorials — they cross two or three categories on purpose. Read the recipe, follow the links into the individual sections to understand each piece, then adapt. If your workflow doesn't appear here, it usually composes from two recipes that do.

items.title: CI: review every PR with claude -p
items.summary: Inline review comments on every pull request, no humans required.
items.details:
Goal: every PR gets an automated review pass before a human touches it.

Ingredients: GitHub Actions, Anthropic's claude-code-action, headless prompt, your project CLAUDE.md.

Steps:
1. Add ANTHROPIC_API_KEY to repo secrets.
2. Create .github/workflows/claude-review.yml (see code).
3. The action checks out the PR, runs Claude Code in headless mode against the diff, and posts comments.
4. Tune the prompt to your codebase: list your project conventions, deny patterns, must-have checks.

Why it works: claude-code-action loads your repo CLAUDE.md so the review enforces your team's actual conventions, not generic advice.

example.code:
name: claude-review
on:
  pull_request:
    types: [opened, synchronize, reopened]

jobs:
  review:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    permissions:
      contents: read
      pull-requests: write
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
        with: { fetch-depth: 0 }
      - uses: anthropics/claude-code-action@v1
        with:
          anthropic_api_key: ${{ secrets.ANTHROPIC_API_KEY }}
          prompt: |
            Review the diff in this PR.
            Flag: secrets, missing tests, breaking API changes, perf hotspots.
            Post inline comments on the offending lines; finish with a short summary.
example.note: Add your CLAUDE.md to the repo first — the action loads it automatically.

items.title: Auto-format every edit
items.summary: Hook formatters into Edit/Write so generated code is always clean.
items.details:
Goal: never see Claude's unformatted output again.

Ingredients: PostToolUse hook, your project's formatter (prettier / ruff / gofmt / etc).

Steps:
1. Add a PostToolUse hook in .claude/settings.json that matches Edit and Write.
2. The hook receives $CLAUDE_FILE_PATH on stdin event JSON; parse it (or use the env var the harness exposes) and run the formatter.
3. Use `2>/dev/null || true` so a missing formatter doesn't break the loop.

Why it works: deterministic — formatting happens whether Claude remembers or not. No prompt engineering needed.

example.note: Swap `prettier` for `ruff format`, `gofmt -w`, etc.

items.title: Generate commit messages from staged diffs
items.summary: Git's prepare-commit-msg hook calls claude -p so commits write themselves.
items.details:
Goal: never type a commit message again. Get a conventional-commit one-liner derived from the actual diff.

Ingredients: a git hook, claude -p, optional: a CLAUDE.md rule for your preferred commit style.

Steps:
1. Drop the script below in .git/hooks/prepare-commit-msg and chmod +x it.
2. Run `git commit` — Claude reads the staged diff and pre-fills the message.
3. Edit before save if needed; Claude's draft is just a starting point.

Why it works: -p inherits CLAUDE.md, so any "commit style" rules you've set apply.

example.code:
# .git/hooks/prepare-commit-msg
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# Skip on rebases / merges / amends with an existing message
[ -n "$2" ] && exit 0

DIFF=$(git diff --staged)
[ -z "$DIFF" ] && exit 0

claude -p "Write a one-line conventional commit for this diff:\n$DIFF" >> "$1"
example.note: chmod +x .git/hooks/prepare-commit-msg

items.title: Block dangerous Bash commands
items.summary: PreToolUse hook + deny rules: rm -rf, force pushes, prod deploys never run accidentally.
items.details:
Goal: even if Claude proposes a destructive command, the harness refuses to execute it.

Ingredients: settings.json deny rules + a PreToolUse hook for anything that needs context-aware checks (e.g., 'rm under $HOME but not in /tmp').

Steps:
1. Start with deny rules — fast, declarative, no scripting.
2. Add a PreToolUse hook for the rest. Exit non-zero to block.
3. Pair with `ask` rules for things that should always prompt regardless of past approvals.

Why it works: deny wins over allow, and hooks fire before the tool runs — there's no way past them.

example.note: Hook script reads JSON from stdin; exit 1 to block.

items.title: Weekly stale-PR triage
items.summary: /schedule a remote agent that pings on stale PRs every Monday morning.
items.details:
Goal: PRs older than 7 days get summarized and re-pinged automatically; nothing rots in the queue.

Ingredients: /schedule + a custom command (.claude/commands/triage-stale-prs.md) that knows your conventions.

Steps:
1. Drop a custom command in .claude/commands/triage-stale-prs.md (see code) describing exactly what 'triage' means in your repo.
2. From any session in that repo, run `/schedule weekly Mondays 9am — /triage-stale-prs`.
3. The cloud-side agent runs on schedule, opens summary issues or pings authors per your instructions.

Why it works: /schedule picks up the same CLAUDE.md and custom commands as your local sessions. Same rules, same voice.

example.code:
# .claude/commands/triage-stale-prs.md
---
allowed-tools: Bash(gh:*), Bash(git:*)
model: sonnet
---

Find all open PRs in this repo with no activity in 7+ days.
For each: post a comment summarizing the diff and tagging @<author>.
Group by team and post a summary issue tagged 'triage'.

Then schedule with:
  /schedule weekly Mondays 9am — /triage-stale-prs
example.note: Manage routines via `/schedule list`.

items.title: Risky refactor in a worktree
items.summary: Spawn a sub-agent on an isolated git worktree so a wild refactor can't break your main branch.
items.details:
Goal: try a brave refactor (upgrade a major dep, rename a core abstraction) without polluting your working tree.

Ingredients: the Agent tool with isolation: "worktree".

Steps:
1. From your main session, ask Claude to spawn a sub-agent with worktree isolation describing the refactor goal.
2. The sub-agent works in a temporary worktree on its own branch — your main tree is untouched.
3. When it returns, inspect the branch with git diff or open it in another editor window.
4. Cherry-pick what works, drop what doesn't.

Why it works: a temp worktree is a real branch + checkout; the agent can run tests, lint, and commit there. You see the actual diff before any of it lands in your main branch.

example.code:
Spawn a sub-agent (worktree-isolated) to:
  1. Upgrade React from 18 → 19
  2. Update all components for the new API
  3. Run tests and lint until clean
  4. Report the diff and a list of files that needed manual review

→ Sub-agent works in a side branch.
→ I review the diff before merging anything.
example.note: isolation: "worktree" is an option on the Agent tool.

categories.title: Power moves
categories.description: Advanced moves that compound — parallel tools, /loop monitoring, /schedule for follow-ups, ultrareview, worktree-isolated agents.
categories.longDescription:
Power moves are the advanced techniques that *compound* — they multiply the leverage of everything else: parallel sub-agents for independent investigations, /loop for polling-style waits, /schedule for follow-up work that fires days or weeks later, ultrareview for multi-agent code review, worktree-isolated agents that won't touch your working tree.

Use this section once the basics are second nature and you want to scale. Common moments: you need five investigations done in parallel and don't want to wait serially; you've shipped a feature flag and want a check-in agent in two weeks to clean up; you want to let a long task chew on its own branch in a worktree while you keep coding on main.

Nothing here changes what Claude *can* do — it changes how much of it you can do at once and how little you have to remember. Pick one technique to try per week and let muscle memory build; these are the tools that turn Claude from a copilot into infrastructure.

items.title: Encourage parallel tool calls
items.summary: Big speedup — Claude can run independent tools concurrently.
items.details: When tasks are independent (multiple reads/greps), Claude can issue several tool calls in one turn. Nudge in CLAUDE.md or by phrasing the request.

example.code:
# CLAUDE.md
## Conventions
- When checks are independent, run them in parallel.

items.title: /ultrareview
items.summary: Multi-agent cloud review of the current branch (or a PR).
items.details: Spawns several reviewers in parallel — security, correctness, style — and aggregates findings. /ultrareview <PR#> for a GitHub PR.

example.note: User-triggered and billed.

items.title: /security-review
items.summary: Sharper safety pass on the current diff.
items.details: Built-in skill that flags secrets, injection vectors, unsafe APIs, and dependency risks.

items.title: /loop for autonomous monitoring
items.summary: Run a prompt or command on a schedule until you stop it.
items.details: Pair with -p style prompts that check status (deploy, queue, alerts) and only ping on change.

example.code: /loop 5m check the canary error rate; ping me only if > 1%
example.note: Stop with /loop-stop.

items.title: /schedule for follow-ups
items.summary: Cron-scheduled remote agents — recurring or one-time.
items.details: Great for cleanup PRs, weekly triage, post-launch checks. Runs in the cloud, opens PRs/issues for you.

examples.code: /schedule once in 14 days — clean up the BETA_ROLLOUT flag
examples.note: One-time follow-up.
Recurring routine.

items.title: Worktree-isolated work
items.summary: Run risky changes in a temporary git worktree.
items.details: Spawn a sub-agent with isolation: "worktree" so changes land in a side branch you can inspect or discard.

example.code: Use a worktree-isolated agent to upgrade React. Report back with the diff.
